coredata.dc:title: Solvothermal conversion of coal into nitrogen-doped carbon dots with singlet oxygen generation and high quantum yield
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Mingyu
Yu, Chang
Hu, Chao
Yang, Wenbo
Zhao, Changtai
Wang, Shuai
Zhang, Mengdi
Zhao, Jianzhang
Wang, Xiuna
Qiu, Jieshan
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are synthesized by a facile one-pot solvothermal approach from Taixi anthracite in the presence of dimethyl formamide (DMF). Benefiting from superior dispersion of light components in DMF and easy exfoliation of smaller sp2-carbon structures from the anthracite, the as-prepared N-CDs show a strong photoluminescence (PL) with a high quantum yield (QY) of 47.0% and production yield of 25.6wt%. Besides, the N-CDs also exhibit a singlet oxygen (1O2) generation behavior with a 1O2 generation QY of 19.0%. Moreover, the as-obtained N-CDs emit blue PL under the excitation wavelength of 365nm, and can be easily dispersed in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), retaining the inherent advantages of the N-CDs and the PMMA matrix. The possible mechanism involved in the formation of N-CDs is proposed and discussed in terms of the macromolecular structure of the anthracite and the solvothermal approach adopted in the study.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal
Anthracite
Carbon
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Preparation
Solvothermal approach
Singlet oxygen generation
Quantum yield
Yield

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs, carbogenic dots and single coal-based CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the N-CDs, the inset is the high-resolution TEM image of the N-CDs. Scale bar, 2nm. (b) Size distribution of N-CDs.
XPS spectra of the as-made N-CDs. (a) Survey spectrum; (b) C 1s spectrum; (c) N 1s spectrum; (d) O 1s spectrum.

(a) UV–vis and PL spectra of the N-CDs, the inset is photographs of the N-CDs solution illuminated under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right, 365nm excitation). (b) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs excited at different wavelengths as indicated.
The diagram of possible mechanism for the synthesis of N-CDs.
Digital photographs of the coal-based N-CDs-PMMA composites under (a) visible and (b) 365nm excitation.
Photophysical parameters of the N-CDs and carbogenic dots measured in DMF.

head.ce:title: Solvothermal conversion of coal into nitrogen-doped carbon dots with singlet oxygen generation and high quantum yield

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are synthesized by a facile one-pot solvothermal approach from Taixi anthracite in the presence of dimethyl formamide (DMF). Benefiting from superior dispersion of light components in DMF and easy exfoliation of smaller sp2-carbon structures from the anthracite, the as-prepared N-CDs show a strong photoluminescence (PL) with a high quantum yield (QY) of 47.0% and production yield of 25.6wt%. Besides, the N-CDs also exhibit a singlet oxygen (1O2) generation behavior with a 1O2 generation QY of 19.0%. Moreover, the as-obtained N-CDs emit blue PL under the excitation wavelength of 365nm, and can be easily dispersed in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), retaining the inherent advantages of the N-CDs and the PMMA matrix. The possible mechanism involved in the formation of N-CDs is proposed and discussed in terms of the macromolecular structure of the anthracite and the solvothermal approach adopted in the study.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of newly emerged fluorescent nanomaterial, have been synthesized from various kinds of carbon sources including fullerene, graphite, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes and carbohydrates by top-down methods [1–9]. Among these carbon sources available now, low-cost and earth-abundant coal has demonstrated a unique and charming superiority [10–13]. For example, Ye et al. have adopted a wet-chemistry route to fabricate graphene quantum dots by heat treatment of coal via acidic oxidation [13]. In our previous work, ethylenediamine-grafted CDs were synthesized by acidic oxidation of coal coupled with surface grafting of the as-obtained CDs, delivering a high quantum yield (QY) [14]. And the size-controlled fluorescent coal-based CDs were also produced by the combined method including carbonization and acidic oxidation etching [12]. However, all of the approaches mentioned above are carried out in the presence of concentrated acid, which are time-consuming, environmentally unfriendly along with a relatively low QY. On one hand, the subdomains formed by acidic oxidation treatment are energetically unfavorable, thus leading to weakly intrinsic emission [15]. On the other hand, the acidic oxidation produces various oxygen-containing functional groups on CDs, which will provoke nonradiative recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus leading to a relatively low QY [16,17].

It should be noted that the defects and free radicals on the surface of CDs can promote the transfer of light energy from CDs to oxygen, finally leading to the generation of reactive oxygen species such as singlet oxygen (1O2) [18]. Such a unique characteristic is capable of extending the practical application of CDs such as in photodynamic therapy field [19]. With this information in mind, the 1O2 generation ability of CDs is highly concerned.

Herein, we firstly synthesized fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) from Taixi anthracite via a facile one-pot solvothermal route in the presence of dimethyl formamide (DMF). The N-CDs can be obtained by rotary evaporation rather than dialysis, and the solvent can be retrieved and recycled, indicative of a simple, green and sustainable process. The as-prepared N-CDs show a strong photoluminescence (PL) with a high QY of 47.0%. Besides, 1O2 generation behavior is also delivered in N-CDs, with a 1O2 generation QY up to 19.0%. The present work is an efficient strategy for fabricating N-CDs with high QY, which will provide a new impetus for practical application of the CDs [20–22].

One kind of Chinese anthracite from Taixi coal mine was used. Its scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image and analysis data are shown in Fig. S1 and Table S1, respectively. The coal sample without pretreatment was crushed and sieved with a 200 mesh sieve, and fully dried before use. DMF was ordered from Tianjin Fuyu Chemical Reagent Factory of China. Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory of China. Methyl methacrylate (MMA) was bought from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Reagent Factory of China. Dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off=3500Da) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were used as received without any further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs, carbogenic dots and single coal-based CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Pristine anthracite was crushed and dispersed in 20mL of DMF with a concentration of 2.5mg/mL. After ultrasonication for 1h, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50mL) and kept at 180°C for 12h. After cooling down, a supernatant brown colloid was achieved by centrifuging at 9000rpm for 30min. The powder of CDs can be obtained by rotary evaporation of the solution, denoted as N-CDs. The comparison experiment was carried out in the absence of the anthracite, yielding the carbogenic dots. Single coal-based CDs were also prepared by chemical oxidation of anthracite following our previous work [12,14].

300mg of AIBN was ultrasonically dispersed into methyl methacrylate (5mL). After adding the N-CDs, the N-CDs-PMMA composite monoliths were synthesized by solidifying at 50°C for 24h, in which the loading fractions of N-CDs in PMMA were controlled to be 0, 2.5, 25, 50, 100, and 200ppm, respectively.

The morphology of coal was characterized by SEM (QUANTA 450 microscope). The structure of CDs was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips Tecnai G220) and high-resolution TEM (TF30). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were scanned by Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Thermofisher UV–vis spectrophotometer and Thermofisher spectrophotometer, respectively. The data of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained using a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with an Al-Kα X-ray source. Raman measurements were carried out on Thermo Fisher Scientific DXR Raman Microscope using laser excitation at 780nm.

QY determination was achieved according to the previously established procedure [23,24]. Rhodamine B (literature QY 0.31 at 340nm) was selected as a reference. The QYs were calculated by comparing the integrated PL intensity (excited at 340nm) and the absorbance value (at 340nm) of the standard with each of the coal-based CDs, based on the following equation:
∅ =
∅
r
×
A
r
I
r
×
I
A
×
n
2
n
r
2
where Φ is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and A is the optical density. The subscript “r” refers to the reference fluorophore of a known QY. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorbance in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette was kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 340nm.

Φ
△ value of the triplet photosensitizer was determined according to a modified literature method with the Ir(ppy)2bpy (Φ
△
= 0.92 in CH2Cl2/CH3OH (9:1, v/v)) as the standard [25]. 1, 3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used as the 1O2 scavenger due to its fast reaction with 1O2. The absorbance of DPBF was adjusted around 1.0 at the maximum absorbance wavelength of 414nm by changing the amount of the air-saturated CH2Cl2/CH3OH solvent. The photosensitizers (Ir(ppy)2bpy and the N-CDs were added to the cuvette, respectively, and the absorbance was adjusted to around 0.2–0.3 by increasing the proportion of the solvent. The crossover wavelength of the photosensitizers (Ir(ppy)2bpy and the N-CDs) was at 355nm. In this case, 355nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength of the samples to exclude the effects of the absorption correction factor (Fig. S2). Then the cuvette was exposed to monochromatic light with an energy of 0.8W/m2 at 355.0nm for 10s. Absorbance was measured after each irradiation (six data points were collected). The slope of the curves for absorbance maxima versus irradiation time was calculated for each photosensitizer. 1O2 generation QY (Φ Δ) was calculated according to the equation
∅
Δ unk
=
∅
Δ std
×
m
unk
m
std
×
F
std
F
unk
where “unk” and “std” designate the “N-CDs photosensitizer” and “Ir(ppy)2bpy”, respectively, “m” is the slope that is calculated by the curve of DPBF absorbance (414nm) versus irradiation time, and “F” is the absorption correction factor, which is given by F=1–10−A (A is absorbance at the irradiation wavelength). For comparison, the control experiments were carried out in the absence of the photosensitizer, aiming to confirm that the 1O2 generation is derived from the photosensitizer rather than the solvent and DPBF effects.

The as-obtained N-CDs show an enhanced PL with a QY as high as 47.0% and production yield of 25.6wt%, which is higher than those of DMF-derived carbogenic dots (QY of 18.6%) and coal-based CDs (0.34%). The as-made N-CDs also feature a 1O2 generation QY up to 19.0% (Fig. S3, Table 1 , Table S2).

The corresponding SEM image of raw anthracite (Fig. S1) reveals that microscale Taixi anthracite has an irregular size and shape distribution. After the solvothermal treatment, the nanoscale N-CDs with a uniform structure and shape distribution are presented (Fig. 1 ). The corresponding size distribution results reveal that the as-made N-CDs feature an average diameter of ca. 4.7nm. The elemental analysis data indicate that it is mainly composed of C, H, and O elements, along with a small quantity of N and S (Table S1).

XPS measurements were performed to investigate the composition and chemical states of the elements in as-made N-CDs. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig.2 a) shows three characteristic peaks located at ca. 284.6, 400.0, and 531.0eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The nitrogen in N-CDs mainly derives from the precursors of coal and DMF. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig.2b) can be deconvoluted into two single peaks that correspond to the CC/CC (284.6eV) and CO/CN (286.0eV), being similar to that of the DMF-derived carbogenic dots (Fig. S4). The N 1s peaks (Fig.2c) centered at 398.7, 399.9, and 401.6eV can be attributed to the pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N, respectively [21,26–30]. The chemical composition of the N-CDs is further confirmed by elemental analysis, and the mass fraction of C, H, and N is about 82.45, 5.62 and 2.55wt%, respectively (Table S3).

The chemical composition and surface states of the as-obtained N-CDs were characterized by the FTIR spectrum, and the detailed results are shown in Fig. 3
[31–33]. The CO, CO and OH vibration modes are common features of CDs, indicative of the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups after the solvothermal treatment. Several bands from 3000 to 2800cm−1 are assigned to the vibration of long hydrocarbon.

The UV−vis spectrum of N-CDs in Fig.4 a shows an absorption band at ca. 270nm, which is attributed to n-π∗ transition of the oxygen-containing functional groups in the N-CDs, and it is consistent with the results of FTIR characterization in Fig. 3 [34–36]. An obvious peak at 310nm can be observed in the PL excitation spectrum in Fig.4a. Besides, the other relatively strong shoulder at the wavelength of 370nm is present. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior (Fig.4b), similar to what has been reported on most of the PL CDs [20,37].

To reveal the unique role of carbon source, the comparison experiments were also carried out in the absence of coal and DMF, respectively. It is found that the DMF can be also converted into carbogenic dots. The carbogenic dots feature a morphology and size distribution similar to that of the coal-based ones (Fig. S5). An excitation-dependent behavior is observed with the maximum PL excitation at ca. 365nm (Fig. S6) [38]. Nevertheless, a relatively low QY of 18.6% is obtained. To further explore the superiority of solvothermal method adopted, CDs derived from coal were prepared by chemical oxidation in the presence of concentrated nitric acid. Single coal-based CDs exhibit a relatively smaller size distribution than that of the samples obtained by solvothermal treatment (Fig. S7), nevertheless, a low QY of 0.34% is delivered. The reason for this would be that strong oxidation leads to abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and less conjugated structure. These oxygen-containing functional groups provoke nonradiative recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus leading to a relatively low QY.

In summary, we have developed a facile one-pot solvothermal chemistry route to prepare highly photoluminescent N-CDs from Taixi coal. Benefiting from superior dispersion of light components in solvent and easy exfoliation of smaller sp2-carbon structures from the anthracite, the as-prepared N-CDs show a strong PL with a 1O2 generation QY of 19.0%, a high QY of 47.0% and production yield of 25.6wt%. The results have shown that the as-made N-CDs are of great potential in photodynamic therapy and bio-imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21522601, U1508201, 51372028), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (DUT16ZD217), and the National Key Research Development Program of China (2016YFB0101201).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2017.03.090.
